metadata.summary: Score agents on three axes: outcome, trajectory, resources. Capture spans using OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions for uniform export to Langfuse, Phoenix, Datadog, or any OTLP backend.

content.title: Trajectory Evaluation with OTel GenAI Spans
content.summary: Score agent runs on three axes simultaneously: outcome (was the answer right?), trajectory (was the path optimal?), and resources (tokens, cost, steps). Capture trajectories using OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions so traces flow uniformly to any OTLP backend.

why.p: LLM agents have a multi-dimensional cost surface. Two agents can both succeed but differ wildly in efficiency. Agent A might solve a task in 3 steps with 8K tokens costing $0.04 in 12 seconds. Agent B might solve the same task in 22 steps with 90K tokens costing $0.45 in 180 seconds. A single "% success" metric scores them identically; the multi-axis view scores B as 11 times worse on resources and 7 times worse on path-length.

OpenTelemetry GenAI spans (gen_ai.* attributes, gen_ai.completion.tokens, gen_ai.system, gen_ai.operation.name) make this measurable across vendors. Each agent step becomes a span; each tool call becomes a child span; the full run becomes a trace. The span attributes are vendor-neutral and standardized, so traces flow uniformly to Langfuse, Phoenix, Datadog, Helicone, or any OTLP backend.

list.item: Production agents where you need to debug regressions
A/B comparing prompts, models, or trajectories
Cost optimization finding which tasks burn the most
Compliance and audit (regulated industries need replayable traces)
Multi-agent systems where you need to know which subagent did what
One-shot personal scripts where instrumenting takes longer than the script
When telemetry would leak PII; redact at the span boundary or use a privacy-preserving collector
When the OTel overhead matters in latency-critical paths (rare; instrumentation is usually less than 1ms per span)

section.name: what-to-capture
section.p: Capture structured data at three scopes: per LLM call, per tool call, and per agent run.

For each LLM call, capture: gen_ai.system (provider like anthropic, openai, google), gen_ai.request.model (exact model name), gen_ai.usage.input_tokens and gen_ai.usage.output_tokens, gen_ai.usage.cache_read_tokens and gen_ai.usage.cache_creation_tokens, gen_ai.response.finish_reason (end_turn, max_tokens, tool_use).
For each tool call, capture: Tool name, Argument hash (for replay), Outcome (success, error, or timeout), Duration.
For the agent run as a whole, capture: Goal (input), Outcome (success, failure, or partial), Total turns, Total cost, Total latency, Final-answer fingerprint (hash for dedup).

antipattern.reason: Add token, step, and latency axes to detect regressions in efficiency
Stick to OTel GenAI conventions; map vendor specifics in attributes

antipattern.description: Tracing the LLM call but not the tool result
antipattern.reason: Tool spans capture the half of the trajectory you actually need to debug
Hash long content; store full content in a side store referenced by hash
Each span should carry enough state to re-run the call
Make eval a CI step; regress on cost just like on correctness

section.name: regression-signals
section.p: Three classes of regression are detectable via trajectory eval. Outcome regression happens when success rate drops. Trajectory regression happens when the number of steps increases or the path becomes less optimal (e.g., tool is called multiple times with the same arguments, indicating loops). Resource regression happens when tokens, cost, or latency spike without improving outcome.

A concrete example: After upgrading from Sonnet 4.5 to 4.6, outcome stayed at 96%, but trajectory score dropped from 0.78 to 0.61: average steps went from 4.1 to 7.3. Root cause: a tool description had been removed during the upgrade. The model now meandered before picking the right tool. Outcome eval alone would have called this a "no regression"; trajectory eval flagged it immediately.

Another example: outcome flat, trajectory flat, but cost score dropped from 0.85 to 0.42—average run cost went from $0.04 to $0.18. Cause: an upstream tool started returning verbose output (10 times longer); each tool result inflated context. Trajectory looked similar but each step was 10 times more expensive. Only the cost axis showed the problem.

section.name: multi-axis-eval
section.p: Build eval around three independent axes. Outcome scorer answers: was the final answer correct? Use rule-based matching for deterministic answers, or LLM-as-judge with a structured rubric for open-ended answers. Trajectory scorer answers: was the path optimal? Count steps, detect loops (same tool called 3+ times with same args), identify wasted work (tool call right after the answer was reachable), and flag unsafe actions (skipping a recommended preview tool). Resource scorer answers: was the solution efficient? Compute normalized cost (1 - min(1, total_cost / max_acceptable_cost)), normalized steps, and latency percentiles.

The three scores form a composite judgment. An agent that is 100% correct but uses 50 times more tokens than necessary is a failing agent. Conversely, an agent that is slightly less correct but uses 1/10 the cost and steps might be preferable depending on the use case. The multi-axis view makes these trade-offs explicit and measurable.

section.name: cache-hit-rate-as-health-metric
section.p: Tracking gen_ai.usage.cache_read_tokens over time reveals system health and points to optimization opportunities. One team noticed cache hit rate was plateauing at 65% despite increasing compute power. Investigation showed a per-user variable was being interpolated into the system prompt, preventing cache reuse across users. Moving the variable to the user message instead of the system prompt raised cache hit rate to 92% and reduced cost per call by 35%. The cache hit rate metric made this optimization discoverable.

section.name: subagent-depth-detection
section.p: A subagent depth metric (how nested are the spans?) serves as a warning signal for decomposition quality. One team found average depth jumped from 1.2 in week 1 to 3.4 in week 4, indicating subagents spawning subagents spawning subagents. Each layer added latency and cost without clear benefit. Flattening the hierarchy reduced p99 latency from 180 seconds to 60 seconds. The span nesting depth is a free metric that catches architectural regressions early.

section.name: composition
section.p: Trajectory eval composes with other methodologies:

list.item: stream-json-orchestration: each stream event becomes one span
subagent-as-context-firewall: subagent runs are nested traces under the parent
prompt-cache-prefix-order: cache_read_tokens shows up in spans—hit-rate monitoring is a side benefit

example.title: Replay-based debugging
example.p: Production failure: an agent emitted nonsense for one specific user. The trace for the failed run showed the exact prompt plus tool calls. Replaying the spans against a sandbox model reproduced the issue (it was a prompt-injection from a CMS field). Fix: input sanitization. The replay capability turned a 4-hour debug session into 20 minutes. Spans carried enough info to re-run the call: model name, prompt hash, tool args.

example.title: LLM-as-judge with structured rubric
example.code:
class Judgment(BaseModel): evidence: list[str] # quotations from agent's output matches_goal: bool completeness_0_to_1: float confidence: Literal["high", "medium", "low"]
judgment = sonnet_judge(trace=trace, rubric=rubric)
example.p: Schema-field-order: evidence first, judgments later—the model has to ground its score in citations, reducing hallucinated scores.

example.title: Exporting to Langfuse
example.p: After this, all OTel spans flow to Langfuse where they're queryable as traces—searchable by attributes like agent.tool.name equals apply_patch.

example.title: Anti-example: token counts not captured
example.p: A team built a custom OTel wrapper that captured agent_step spans but didn't fill in token counts. Cost analytics were impossible; the only visible metric was duration. Instrumentation cost: approximately 30 lines of code; benefit lost: approximately $5K per month overspend that took 3 months to surface via the cloud bill.

template.purpose: Python template for instrumenting Anthropic calls with OTel
Python template for instrumenting tool calls with OTel
Eval rubric template scoring outcome, trajectory, resources

checklist.check: Every LLM call wrapped in a span with gen_ai.system, gen_ai.request.model
Token counts captured: gen_ai.usage.input_tokens, gen_ai.usage.output_tokens, cache read and create
gen_ai.response.finish_reason recorded
Every tool call wrapped in a child span with name, args hash, outcome, duration
Agent run wrapped in a root span with goal, outcome, totals
Subagent spans nested under parent agent span
Eval scores on 3 axes minimum: outcome, trajectory, resources
Outcome scorer: rule-based or LLM-as-judge with structured rubric
Trajectory scorer: count steps, detect loops, identify wasted work
Resource scorer: tokens, cost, latency, cache hit rate
Eval runs in CI on every prompt, model, tool change
OTLP exporter configured (gRPC or HTTP)
Backend chosen: Langfuse, Phoenix, Datadog, Honeycomb, or self-hosted Jaeger
PII redaction at the collector boundary
Trace retention policy defined (typical: 7-30 days hot, longer in archive)
Spans carry enough info to replay the call (model, prompt hash, tool args)
Replay tooling exists for at least the LLM-call spans
Replay used for regression debugging (not just for live ops)
Cache hit rate tracked over time as a regression signal
Subagent depth tracked (deeper hierarchies often indicate poor decomposition)
Cost per task type tracked (find expensive task templates and target optimization)
